baseline_dominance.justification: The construction identifies real gaps (hidden-bitmask predicate evaluation, operator-correlation resistance via blindingFactor) that the baseline cannot match. However, Tier 3 attacks materially weaken several of the six claimed gaps: rfc7662_advocate Attack 2 shows RFC 9068 + cached JWKS already achieves AS-blind presentation (Gap 1 is misframed as roundtrip avoidance rather than trust-anchor difference); Attack 4 shows RFC 8693 token exchange achieves runtime-adaptive scope restriction (Gap 2's uniqueness is only the hidden-bitmask sub-property, not runtime adaptivity per se); spiffe_engineer Attack 3 shows SPIFFE federation already achieves adversarial-AS resilience without blockchain state (Gap 3 conflates AS-independence with scope privacy). The genuine irreducible differentiator is hidden-bitmask predicate evaluation with operator-correlation resistance — but the construction frames six gaps when only two survive scrutiny. Baseline can approximate the others with extra configuration, so this is preferable but not provably exclusive across all claimed dimensions.

formal_security_argument.justification: The construction provides explicit SSU and SP game definitions, names six cryptographic assumptions (A1–A6), and gives reduction sketches for both games. This is substantially above average. However, cryptographer Attack 4 identifies that A5 (Poseidon PRF assumption) has no formal game definition — it is stated informally and is load-bearing in the SP proof. The construction conflates PRF, one-time PRF, and commitment-with-hiding properties. Cryptographer Attack 3 identifies that Groth16 is not simulation-extractable, and the composition of two Groth16 proofs (EnrollmentIntegrity + AgentPolicy) requires SE or an explicit AGM argument that the construction does not provide. Cryptographer Attack 1 notes two Phase 2 ceremonies with no ceremony specification, meaning A1's premise (unsubverted CRS) is unsubstantiated. The threat model is stated, assumptions are named, but the reduction has identifiable gaps (no A5 game, no SE/AGM clause, no ceremony spec). Score 1: threat model stated but assumption vague or reduction incomplete.

implementability.justification: The construction maps precisely to existing Bolyra primitives: Poseidon hash (2/3/4/5 variants), Groth16 with pot16.ptau, PLONK, BabyJubjub EdDSA, BinaryMerkleRoot(20), Num2Bits(64). Constraint counts are itemized: AgentPolicy ~21,139, EnrollmentIntegrity ~8,131 — both well under the 2^16 = 65,536 pot16.ptau ceiling. Proving time targets are stated (< 0.5s rapidsnark, < 5s WASM for AgentPolicy; < 0.2s rapidsnark for EnrollmentIntegrity). The EnrollmentIntegrity circuit uses only existing gadgets (Poseidon5, Poseidon2, EdDSAPoseidonVerifier, Num2Bits). All within Phase 1 budget. The auth0_pm latency attack (Attack 2) questions whether benchmarks are measured vs. targeted, but the cost is stated and within budget.

adversarial_survival.justification: Tier 3 codex adversary and 5 personas attacked the construction extensively. Several attacks land squarely outside the threat model and weaken claims without being fully mitigated: (1) cryptographer Attack 2 — adaptive chosen-requiredScopeMask attack recovers full permissionBitmask in 64 rounds via proof acceptance/refusal oracle, SP game does not model adversarial RS choosing different masks; (2) cryptographer Attack 4 — A5 has no formal PRF game, load-bearing in SP proof; (3) cu_ciso Attack 1 — privacy-as-feature contradicts NCUA Part 748 audit trail requirements, construction claims regulatory compliance but architecturally prevents audit reconstruction; (4) spiffe_engineer Attack 4 — blindingFactor bootstrap problem where operator controls agent runtime and can extract blindingFactor, breaking the SP game's core assumption in realistic deployments; (5) rfc7662_advocate Attack 1 — adversarial-AS enrolls inflated permissions, reducing adversarial-AS resilience to same trust anchor as RFC 7662. Multiple real attacks weaken claims. Score 1: Tier 3 surfaced real attacks that the construction mitigates partially but weaken several core claims.

scenario_fit.justification: The CUSO federated credit union scenario (§7) is concrete: named stakeholder (CUSO with 200 member CUs), named regulation (NCUA §701.27, GLBA §501(b)), specific operational flow. However, cu_ciso Attack 1 demonstrates that the SP game's privacy guarantee directly contradicts NCUA Part 748 Appendix B audit trail requirements — the construction architecturally prevents the activity reconstruction that examiners require. cu_ciso Attack 2 shows blindingFactor custody is unspecified, failing GLBA §314.4(c). cu_ciso Attack 3 shows the 30-root buffer creates an unspecified revocation window failing FFIEC CAT D3.CC.1. The scenario is named and specific, but the regulatory claims within it are contested by the attacks — the construction's privacy guarantee is structurally opposed to the audit requirements of the very regulations it cites. The scenario is real but the property is not clearly load-bearing in the way claimed; it may actually be a liability under the cited regulations. Score 1: scenario named but regulatory fit is contested.

gaps: A5 (Poseidon PRF) needs a formal game definition covering the joint-input structure where the adversary sees both Poseidon2(credCommitment, blind) and Poseidon3(credCommitment, blind, nonce) — currently a hand-wave that is load-bearing in the SP proof

Adaptive chosen-requiredScopeMask attack: a malicious RS recovers the full 64-bit permissionBitmask in 64 rounds via proof acceptance/refusal oracle — SP game must model adversarial RS choosing different masks across sessions, or the privacy claim must be explicitly scoped to passive single-mask RSes

Phase 2 ceremony specification missing for both AgentPolicy and EnrollmentIntegrity circuits — number of participants, transcript format, public auditability, and recovery procedure if compromise is discovered must be defined

blindingFactor custody and lifecycle unspecified — storage requirements (HSM/enclave), backup/recovery, rotation on decommission, and remediation on disclosure are absent, breaking the SP game's core assumption in realistic operator-controlled deployments

Regulatory audit trail contradiction: SP game's privacy guarantee architecturally prevents NCUA Part 748 Appendix B audit reconstruction — construction must define a selective deanonymization mechanism (blindingFactor escrow, threshold disclosure, or agent-written encrypted audit log) or drop the NCUA compliance claim

Revocation SLA unspecified: 30-root history buffer creates an undefined post-revocation validity window (60s to 5+ hours depending on root update frequency) — must define root update frequency, maximum revocation latency, and emergency invalidation path for high-privilege agents
AS-blind framing in Gap 1 is defeated by RFC 9068 + cached JWKS — reframe as AS-trust-anchor difference (consensus-secured Merkle root vs. AS signing key) rather than roundtrip avoidance

Adversarial-AS enrollment inflation: operator can sign credentials with permissionBitmask=0xFF for an agent that should hold READ_DATA only — adversarial-AS resilience claim must be bounded to post-enrollment forgery, not enrollment correctness

Groth16 simulation-extractability gap: composition of EnrollmentIntegrity and AgentPolicy proofs requires SE or explicit AGM argument — cite Fuchsbauer et al. 2020 and state A1 in the algebraic group model
